defaults.permitted_development: Some house extensions can be permitted development in England under Class A, but the answer depends on whether the proposal is rear, side or two storey, whether it stays behind the principal elevation and whether the house remains within the 50% curtilage limit. On tighter plots and established suburban streets, daylight, privacy and parking layout are often the real deciding factors.

rules.height_rules: Height must stay within the Class A limits, with special care over eaves near boundaries and any two-storey work that approaches the existing roof line.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: Boundaries are often the deciding factor. Close side passages, shallow rear gardens, corner plots and upper-floor windows can turn an apparently modest extension into one needing permission. On tighter plots and established suburban streets, daylight, privacy and parking layout are often the real deciding factors.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the permitted development route.
rules.materials_rules: External materials should be similar in appearance to the original house so the extension feels like part of the same building rather than a separate structure. On tighter plots and established suburban streets, daylight, privacy and parking layout are often the real deciding factors.

rules.permitted_development: Some house extensions can be permitted development in England under Class A, but the answer depends on whether the proposal is rear, side or two storey, whether it stays behind the principal elevation and whether the house remains within the 50% curtilage limit.

rules.height_rules: Height must stay within the Class A limits, with special care over eaves near boundaries and any two-storey work that approaches the existing roof line.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: Boundaries are often the deciding factor. Close side passages, shallow rear gardens, corner plots and upper-floor windows can turn an apparently modest extension into one needing permission.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: External materials should be similar in appearance to the original house so the extension feels like part of the same building rather than a separate structure.

restrictions.conservation_area: Extensions in a conservation area often need a tighter design and siting assessment, especially on visible elevations or where the scheme would alter roof form, frontage or the rhythm of the street.
restrictions.listed_building: Do not rely on standard householder permitted development rights for a listed building. Listed building consent is usually required for works affecting character, and planning permission may also be needed.
restrictions.article4_notes: Article 4 directions can remove the usual householder fallback route in selected streets or heritage areas, so check the property's designation before relying on permitted development.

rules.permitted_development: England allows some house extensions as Class A permitted development, but only where the project stays within the relevant rear, side or two-storey limits and does not push the enlarged house beyond the normal curtilage allowance.

rules.height_rules: The height test depends on the form of extension, but the pressure points are usually the eaves near boundaries and any upper-storey element that starts to compete with the existing roof and eaves.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: Boundary relationships often decide whether a mixed house-extension scheme remains PD. Tight side paths, short back gardens and upper-floor windows can all push the proposal out of the easy fallback route.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: Match the wall, roof and joinery finishes closely to the original house so the extension reads as a natural addition rather than a detached-looking block.

restrictions.conservation_area: Listed buildings, conservation areas, historic parks, gardens, and ancient monuments
restrictions.listed_building: Listed buildings, conservation areas, historic parks, gardens, and ancient monuments
restrictions.article4_notes: Article 4 directions can remove the usual householder fallback route in selected streets or heritage areas, so check the property's designation before relying on permitted development.

rules.permitted_development: A house extension may stay within permitted development in England where it fits the correct Class A route for its type, remains behind the principal elevation and keeps enough undeveloped curtilage around the house.

rules.height_rules: Keep single-storey work within the normal Class A height limits and treat any upper-storey element cautiously near the existing roof line and neighbouring boundaries.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: Even where the footprint looks modest, boundary effects can be the issue that matters most. Side clearances, rear garden depth and overlooking from upper windows all need checking early.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: Use materials that closely relate to the original house. The more the extension reads as a separate add-on, the weaker the design case usually becomes.

rules.permitted_development: A house extension can still fall within Class A in England, but in parts of this authority Article 4 or heritage controls may narrow that fallback route. Even where rights remain, the extension still has to satisfy the type-specific depth, width, height and curtilage limits.

rules.height_rules: The same Class A height rules still apply in sensitive streets: watch the eaves near boundaries and avoid upper-storey work that starts to overtake the existing roof line.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: In tighter or heritage-sensitive streets, councils often look especially hard at side clearances, rear garden depth and upper-floor overlooking, even where a PD argument is being made.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: Close material matching is especially important in heritage-sensitive areas so the extension reads as restrained work to the existing house.

rules.materials_rules: Match the wall, roof and joinery finishes closely to the original house so the extension reads as a natural addition rather than a detached-looking block.

restrictions.conservation_area: Extensions in a conservation area often need a tighter design and siting assessment, especially on visible elevations or where the scheme would alter roof form, frontage or the rhythm of the street.
restrictions.listed_building: Do not rely on standard householder permitted development rights for a listed building. Listed building consent is usually required for works affecting character, and planning permission may also be needed.
restrictions.article4_notes: Article 4 directions can remove the usual householder fallback route in selected streets or heritage areas, so check the property's designation before relying on permitted development.

rules.permitted_development: A house extension may stay within permitted development in England where it fits the correct Class A route for its type, remains behind the principal elevation and keeps enough undeveloped curtilage around the house.

rules.height_rules: Keep single-storey work within the normal Class A height limits and treat any upper-storey element cautiously near the existing roof line and neighbouring boundaries.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: Even where the footprint looks modest, boundary effects can be the issue that matters most. Side clearances, rear garden depth and overlooking from upper windows all need checking early.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: Use materials that closely relate to the original house. The more the extension reads as a separate add-on, the weaker the design case usually becomes.

restrictions.conservation_area: Extensions in a conservation area often need a tighter design and siting assessment, especially on visible elevations or where the scheme would alter roof form, frontage or the rhythm of the street.
restrictions.listed_building: Do not rely on standard householder permitted development rights for a listed building. Listed building consent is usually required for works affecting character, and planning permission may also be needed.
restrictions.article4_notes: Article 4 directions can remove the usual householder fallback route in selected streets or heritage areas, so check the property's designation before relying on permitted development.

rules.permitted_development: Some house extensions can be permitted development in England under Class A, but the answer depends on whether the proposal is rear, side or two storey, whether it stays behind the principal elevation and whether the house remains within the 50% curtilage limit.

rules.height_rules: Height must stay within the Class A limits, with special care over eaves near boundaries and any two-storey work that approaches the existing roof line.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: Boundaries are often the deciding factor. Close side passages, shallow rear gardens, corner plots and upper-floor windows can turn an apparently modest extension into one needing permission.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: External materials should be similar in appearance to the original house so the extension feels like part of the same building rather than a separate structure.

restrictions.conservation_area: Search current and historic planning application records. Find information on planning policy, conservation areas, trees and listed buildings. Submit planning permission and building regulations applications.
restrictions.listed_building: Search current and historic planning application records. Find information on planning policy, conservation areas, trees and listed buildings. Submit planning permission and building regulations applications.
restrictions.article4_notes: Article 4 directions can remove the usual householder fallback route in selected streets or heritage areas, so check the property's designation before relying on permitted development.

rules.permitted_development: A house extension can still fall within Class A in England, but in parts of this authority Article 4 or heritage controls may narrow that fallback route. Even where rights remain, the extension still has to satisfy the type-specific depth, width, height and curtilage limits.

rules.height_rules: The same Class A height rules still apply in sensitive streets: watch the eaves near boundaries and avoid upper-storey work that starts to overtake the existing roof line.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: In tighter or heritage-sensitive streets, councils often look especially hard at side clearances, rear garden depth and upper-floor overlooking, even where a PD argument is being made.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: Close material matching is especially important in heritage-sensitive areas so the extension reads as restrained work to the existing house.

restrictions.conservation_area: Planning information about listed buildings, houses in conservation areas and urban design and archaeology.
restrictions.listed_building: Planning information about listed buildings, houses in conservation areas and urban design and archaeology.
restrictions.article4_notes: Article 4 directions apply in parts of this authority and can remove the usual householder fallback route, so confirm the property's designation before treating the extension as permitted development.

rules.materials_rules: Use materials that closely relate to the original house. The more the extension reads as a separate add-on, the weaker the design case usually becomes.

restrictions.conservation_area: We are proud of Newham’s heritage and have a number of conservation areas, listed, and locally listed buildings that are given particular protection for their architectural and historical interest. Find out about these here, along with detail of any additional permissions you may need to conduct building works.
restrictions.listed_building: We are proud of Newham’s heritage and have a number of conservation areas, listed, and locally listed buildings that are given particular protection for their architectural and historical interest. Find out about these here, along with detail of any additional permissions you may need to conduct building works.
restrictions.article4_notes: Article 4 directions can remove the usual householder fallback route in selected streets or heritage areas, so check the property's designation before relying on permitted development.

rules.permitted_development: England allows some house extensions as Class A permitted development, but only where the project stays within the relevant rear, side or two-storey limits and does not push the enlarged house beyond the normal curtilage allowance.

rules.height_rules: The height test depends on the form of extension, but the pressure points are usually the eaves near boundaries and any upper-storey element that starts to compete with the existing roof and eaves.
rules.depth_rules: Depth limits vary by extension type. Rear extensions have the clearest metre limits, while side and wraparound forms are judged more by proportion, width and relationship to the original house.
rules.boundary_rules: Boundary relationships often decide whether a mixed house-extension scheme remains PD. Tight side paths, short back gardens and upper-floor windows can all push the proposal out of the easy fallback route.
rules.roof_rules: The roof should remain subordinate to the host dwelling. Front-facing elements, bulky side roofs, balconies and terrace-style forms are where householder schemes often leave the PD route.
rules.materials_rules: Match the wall, roof and joinery finishes closely to the original house so the extension reads as a natural addition rather than a detached-looking block.
